name: Japanese state forms: Nara → Meiji Empire
description: Seven-link trunk of Japanese state forms seeded in AtlasPI, from the first permanent capital at Heijō-kyō (Nara, 710) through the Heian aristocratic regime, the three successive shogunates (Kamakura, Muromachi, Tokugawa), the brief Azuchi-Momoyama period of reunification, and the Meiji Restoration that refounded the state as the Empire of Japan in 1868. Each transition is typed and annotated: the imperial house remained nominally sovereign throughout while actual power shifted repeatedly between court aristocrats, military governments (bakufu), and, after 1868, the Meiji oligarchs operating through the restored imperial institution.
ethical_notes: The chain presents state-form succession, not unbroken constitutional continuity. Two points of care. (a) The Meiji Restoration of 1868 is often framed as a 'restoration' of direct imperial rule; in practice it was a coup by Satsuma-Chōshū domain leaders against the Tokugawa shogunate, followed by the Boshin War (1868-69, ~8,000 combat dead), the suppression of the Republic of Ezo, and the subsequent annexation of the Ryūkyū Kingdom (1879) and of Ainu Mosir (the Hokkaido colonisation policy from 1869 onward, with Ainu land expropriations and forced assimilation). AtlasPI records Meiji as REVOLUTION rather than RESTORATION because the rupture in fiscal, military, and constitutional structure was total. (b) The seven-link format omits fragmented or short-lived polities (the Nanboku-chō Northern/Southern Courts split 1336-92, the Sengoku warring states 1467-1568) for DB-coverage reasons; those are real discontinuities that should be refined in later batches.

sources.citation: Totman, C. (2000). A History of Japan. Blackwell.
Hall, J.W. (ed.) (1988-99). The Cambridge History of Japan, 6 vols. Cambridge UP.
Jansen, M.B. (2000). The Making of Modern Japan. Harvard UP.
Gluck, C. (1985). Japan's Modern Myths: Ideology in the Late Meiji Period. Princeton UP.

links.description: Emperor Kanmu moves the capital from Heijō-kyō (Nara) to Heian-kyō (Kyōto) in 794 to escape the political influence of the great Buddhist monasteries. The move inaugurates the Heian period and the ascendancy of the Fujiwara regent-family (sesshō and kampaku) over the imperial court.
links.ethical_notes: The capital move is bloodless but is preceded by the Hoki purges and the earlier Dōkyō affair (764-70); political violence in the Nara court is erased in most survey narratives. The move also intensified ritsuryō-system taxation of peripheral populations (notably the Emishi of northeastern Honshū), leading to decades of military campaigns (Sakanoue no Tamuramaro, 780s-810s) with heavy civilian casualties.

links.description: Minamoto no Yoritomo's victory in the Gempei War (1180-85) — climaxing at the naval battle of Dan-no-ura (25 April 1185) where the child emperor Antoku drowned and the Taira clan was annihilated — ends the Heian aristocratic order. The Kamakura shogunate (established 1192 with Yoritomo as sei-i taishōgun) institutes a dual government: the imperial court retains ceremonial authority in Kyōto, real power lies with the bakufu in Kamakura.
links.ethical_notes: The Gempei War involved mass killings of the Taira nobility including women and children (the Dan-no-ura suicides, the executions of captured Taira at Rokujō riverbed in 1185). Yoritomo subsequently purged his own brothers Noriyori and Yoshitsune — the latter hunted down and driven to suicide at Koromogawa in 1189. The shogunal order was consolidated through violence, not constitutional succession.

links.description: Ashikaga Takauji, after betraying the Kemmu Restoration of Emperor Go-Daigo (1333-36), establishes a new shogunate at Kyōto (Muromachi district) and enthrones a rival Northern Court emperor, triggering the Nanboku-chō period (1336-92) in which two imperial lines claimed legitimacy simultaneously.
links.ethical_notes: The Muromachi transition is violent both in its origin (Takauji's betrayal of Go-Daigo; battle of Minatogawa 1336, death of Kusunoki Masashige) and in its eventual collapse (Ōnin War 1467-77 — the destruction of much of Kyōto, ~120,000 dead, opening the Sengoku period). The 'two emperors' problem of 1336-92 is a permanent crack in the official narrative of unbroken imperial lineage.

links.description: Oda Nobunaga enters Kyōto with the Ashikaga shogunal pretender Yoshiaki in 1568, effectively ending Muromachi authority, then deposes Yoshiaki in 1573. The Azuchi-Momoyama period (1568-1600) under Nobunaga, then Toyotomi Hideyoshi, reunifies Japan after a century of Sengoku civil war and launches two invasions of Korea (Imjin War, 1592-98).
links.ethical_notes: Nobunaga's unification included the massacre of the Ikkō-ikki at Nagashima (1574, ~20,000 killed) and the burning of the Enryakuji monastery on Mount Hiei (1571, ~3,000-4,000 monks and laypeople killed). Hideyoshi's Korean invasions caused catastrophic civilian casualties in Korea (estimates 1-2 million dead from combat, famine, and disease) and the mass deportation of Korean artisans, some of whose descendants (notably the Satsuma potter communities) remained in Japan. The reunification narrative must not erase these.

links.description: Tokugawa Ieyasu's decisive victory at the battle of Sekigahara (21 October 1600, ~40,000 killed on both sides) ends the Toyotomi-allied coalition under Ishida Mitsunari. Ieyasu is named sei-i taishōgun in 1603, founding the Tokugawa bakufu at Edo (modern Tōkyō) which will rule for 265 years of internal peace (Pax Tokugawa).
links.ethical_notes: The Tokugawa consolidation included the siege of Ōsaka (1614-15, ~60,000 killed, extermination of the Toyotomi house), the suppression of Christianity (1612 onward), the Shimabara Rebellion massacre (1637-38, ~37,000 Christian peasants killed and Christianity effectively extirpated), the sakoku exclusion edicts closing Japan to most foreign contact, and the rigid status system binding peasants, outcastes (eta, hinin), and the Ainu of Hokkaido to hereditary disability. Internal peace for the samurai order was purchased with structural violence against religious minorities and subordinated populations.

links.description: The Meiji Restoration: Satsuma and Chōshū domain forces overthrow the Tokugawa shogunate after Tokugawa Yoshinobu's formal resignation in November 1867 and the Boshin War (January 1868 - June 1869). The 15-year-old Emperor Meiji is restored to nominal direct rule on 3 January 1868; the capital is moved from Kyōto to Edo (renamed Tōkyō) in 1869. The restored empire launches a rapid industrialisation and military-modernisation programme (the 1868 Charter Oath, 1871 abolition of domains, 1873 conscription law, 1889 Meiji Constitution).
links.ethical_notes: The Meiji transition is recorded as REVOLUTION, not RESTORATION, because the break in constitutional, fiscal, and military structure was total. The Boshin War killed ~8,000. The subsequent Meiji state launched the Hokkaido colonisation (1869-) with Ainu land dispossession and the Disposition of the Ryūkyū Kingdom (1879, forced annexation), and by 1894-95 had begun imperial expansion — the Sino-Japanese War, Taiwan colony 1895, Korea protectorate 1905 and annexation 1910. The Meiji framework is foundational both for Japanese modernisation and for early 20th-century imperial violence in East Asia.

name: North Indian classical successions: Shishunaga → Nanda → Maurya → Shunga → Kanva
description: Five-link dynastic trunk of the classical Magadhan core in the Gangetic plain, spanning roughly 413 BCE to 28 BCE, all centred on Pāṭaliputra (modern Patna) as imperial capital. The Shishunaga dynasty (413-345 BCE) re-established Magadhan power over Avanti; the Nanda dynasty (345-322 BCE) extended rule to a first pan-North Indian hegemony and accumulated the fiscal base that Chandragupta Maurya then seized; the Maurya Empire (322-185 BCE) under Chandragupta, Bindusara, and Aśoka achieved the first subcontinent-scale empire; the Shunga dynasty (185-73 BCE), founded by the regicide Pushyamitra, continued Brahmanical restoration; the Kanva dynasty (73-28 BCE) held a reduced Magadhan kernel until absorbed by the Satavahanas. Each transition here involved assassination or coup — none was a peaceful succession.
ethical_notes: Four points of care. (a) Dates for all five dynasties are contested; the Puranic king-lists and Buddhist-Jain chronologies diverge by 10-50 years, and the Maurya chronology depends on synchronisms with the Hellenistic Greek sources (Chandragupta = Sandrokottos in Greek sources meeting Alexander and Seleucus). AtlasPI uses conventional revised chronology (Thapar, Allchin). (b) The Shunga foundation involved the regicide of the last Maurya (Brihadratha) by his own commander Pushyamitra — recorded plainly in the transition, not rationalised as reform. (c) The Shunga period is traditionally treated in some Buddhist sources as a period of anti-Buddhist persecution (Divyavadana account of Pushyamitra destroying stupas); academic consensus (Thapar, Lahiri) treats this as partly legendary but notes the shift from Aśoka's dhamma-state to Brahmanical orthodoxy was substantive. (d) Vasudeva Kanva's coup against the last Shunga (also Devabhuti) is another regicide, poorly documented. The chain shows that 'classical' North Indian statecraft was politically violent throughout; the Mauryan peace under Aśoka is the anomaly, not the norm.

sources.citation: Thapar, R. (2003). The Penguin History of Early India. Penguin.
Thapar, R. (1997). Aśoka and the Decline of the Mauryas. 2nd ed. Oxford UP.
Lahiri, N. (2015). Ashoka in Ancient India. Harvard UP.
Allchin, F.R. (1995). The Archaeology of Early Historic South Asia. Cambridge UP.
Puranas (Matsya, Vayu, Brahmanda) — dynastic king-lists (primary, redacted c. 300-600 CE).

links.description: Mahapadma Nanda overthrows the last Shishunaga ruler (traditionally Nandivardhana or Mahānandin in Puranic sources). Mahapadma is described by the Puranas as a śūdra of humble origin — the caste-transgressive founding of the Nanda dynasty is remarked on in multiple source traditions. Under the Nandas, Magadhan power reaches its pre-Mauryan peak with a reportedly vast standing army (the Greek accounts of Xandrames's forces facing Alexander's camp are probably Nanda forces).
links.ethical_notes: Puranic hostility to the Nandas (described as 'low-born' and impious) colours the sources; Jain and Buddhist traditions are somewhat less hostile. The characterisation of Mahapadma's usurpation as regicide is consistent across traditions even if the names and details differ.

links.description: Chandragupta Maurya, advised by the Brahmin Kautilya (Chanakya), overthrows Dhana Nanda and founds the Maurya dynasty. The traditional Arthashastra tradition portrays the overthrow as carefully engineered — fiscal subversion of Nanda forts, recruitment of tribal militias in the frontier, then direct attack on Pāṭaliputra. Chandragupta then pushes west against the Seleucid empire (treaty with Seleucus ~305 BCE, transfer of Arachosia, Gedrosia, Paropamisadae to the Mauryans in exchange for 500 war elephants), establishing the first truly subcontinental empire.
links.ethical_notes: The Chandragupta-Chanakya narrative is preserved largely in the Arthashastra (whose authorship and dating are disputed — Olivelle 2013 dates the received text to c. 50-125 CE), in the Mudrarakshasa drama (c. 4th-5th CE), and in the Puranas. Greek sources (Megasthenes's Indica, fragments via Strabo and Arrian) independently attest Sandrokottos / Chandragupta at Pāṭaliputra. The conquest itself is historical; the colourful biographical detail (Chanakya's oath, the poison-maiden stories) is largely legendary.

links.description: Pushyamitra Shunga, commander-in-chief of the last Maurya emperor Brihadratha, assassinates his sovereign during a military review (described in the Harshacharita and Puranas) and founds the Shunga dynasty. The regicide marks the end of Mauryan Buddhist-dhamma-oriented kingship and the restoration of Brahmanical ritual authority (Pushyamitra is credited with performing the Ashvamedha horse-sacrifice, the emblematic Vedic imperial rite).
links.ethical_notes: The regicide is recorded uniformly across sources. Buddhist literature (Divyavadana, Ashokavadana) portrays Pushyamitra as an anti-Buddhist persecutor who offered a bounty for Buddhist monks' heads; academic treatment (Lamotte 1958, Thapar 1997) considers this exaggerated Buddhist polemic but accepts that patronage of Buddhist institutions declined sharply. The Shunga period did see significant Brahmanical temple construction (Bharhut stupa, however, is itself Buddhist and flourished under Shunga rule — so the anti-Buddhist narrative is partial at best).

links.description: Vasudeva Kanva, a minister of the last Shunga ruler Devabhuti, assassinates his king (Puranas depict Devabhuti as dissolute and say Vasudeva used a woman disguised as the queen to gain access to him) and founds the short-lived Kanva dynasty, ruling a reduced Magadha kernel. The Kanvas rule four generations (~45 years) before being overthrown by the Satavahana king Simuka circa 28 BCE — ending classical Magadhan imperial succession.
links.ethical_notes: The Kanva chronology rests almost entirely on the Puranic king-lists; independent epigraphic attestation is weak. The regicide-succession pattern — which by now defines the Magadhan classical trunk — is consistent, and the chain deliberately does not euphemise it as 'succession'. After the Satavahana absorption, Magadhan political centrality does not recover until the Gupta revival four centuries later (320 CE).
